serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of sweetened breakfast cereal is approximately 40 g (about one bowl), often consumed with milk or a plant-based alternative.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal, ready-to-eat, corn/wheat/rice/oat based, sweetened — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cereal made from grains (corn/wheat/rice/oats), record ID F05050001. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened ready-to-eat breakfast cereal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 74 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened ready-to-eat breakfast cereals (corn/wheat/rice-based). This is an analogous estimate as the exact HPB F05050001 formulation is not individually listed in the GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (74 × 33.77) / 100 ≈ 24.9, using a 40 g serving providing 33.77 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (31.2 g/100g) with low fibre (1.6 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 335 mg/100g with negligible potassium (6 mg/100g) worsens the sodium-potassium imbalance
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sweetened cereal due to its high sugar content (31.2 g/100g) and unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; substitute with plain oats or bran-based cereals with fibre above 3 g/100g and sugar below 5 g/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~74), very high carbohydrate (84.4 g/100g), high sugar (31.2 g/100g), and low dietary fibre (1.6 g/100g) produce rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This cereal is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, restrict to a maximum 30 g portion paired with a protein source (e.g. Greek yogurt or eggs) to partially blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (31.2 g/100g) promotes dyslipidaemia, hepatic triglyceride synthesis, and endothelial inflammation; sodium at 335 mg/100g contributes to blood pressure load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sweetened cereal due to its high sugar content promoting triglyceride elevation and its moderate sodium load; choose unsweetened whole-grain alternatives with soluble fibre (e.g. oat bran) to support LDL reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (31.2 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha and IL-6; refined grain base with minimal fibre amplifies systemic glycaemic load and inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened cereal as its high sugar content is a primary driver of inflammatory flares; replace with plain oats or muesli with nuts and seeds to provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweetened grain-based breakfast cereal (HPB Singapore record F05050001) is a ready-to-eat, ultra-processed cereal product made from one or more of the following grains: corn, wheat, rice, or oats. It is typically consumed as a breakfast food, served dry or with milk or a plant-based alternative. The product is characterised by a high carbohydrate content (84.4 g/100g), of which a substantial proportion is added sugar (31.2 g/100g), giving it a sweet flavour and a crisp or flake-like texture. Dietary fibre is low at 1.6 g/100g, and protein is modest at 5.2 g/100g. Fat content is 4.9 g/100g. Sodium is 335 mg/100g, which is borderline-high for a breakfast food. Iron is notably elevated at 5.44 mg/100g, likely due to fortification. Energy density is high at 407 kcal/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 74 (high), and a 40 g serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 24.9, making this food clinically significant for blood glucose management. This cereal is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, high glycaemic load, and low fibre profile. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable but contains gluten. Commonly consumed at breakfast in Singapore and internationally.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in cereal?
Is cereal good for weight loss?
What is the glycemic index of cereal?
How many calories in a bowl of cereal?
Can I eat cereal if I have heart disease?
What is a healthy alternative to sweetened cereal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the canonical HPB Singapore Foodo database record F05050001 with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for the same food category.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this generic cereal record (F05050001) as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the HPB record. potassium_mg is reported as 6.0 mg/100g per HPB, which is negligibly low. GI of 74 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened ready-to-eat breakfast cereals; the exact HPB F05050001 formulation is not individually listed. Glycemic load per 40 g serving = (74 × 33.77) / 100 ≈ 24.9. The dietary_tags 'wholegrain' from the input has been retained in classification context; however, the nutritional profile (low fibre, high sugar) is inconsistent with a true wholegrain product and may reflect a mixed-grain formulation. No local-language names are required as this is a generic Western/international food category with no mandatory Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese language requirements under G6.
